nodes.title: Test continuity of a function at a point
nodes.brief: Decide whether f is continuous at c by checking the limit at c equals f(c).
nodes.quote: Then f is continuous at c if
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Continuity at c requires limit = value.
f is continuous at a point c in its domain if lim(x->c) f(x) = f(c). Three things must hold: the left-hand limit, the right-hand limit, and f(c) must all exist and be EQUAL to one another. If any fails, f is discontinuous at c.

PART 2 — Check both one-sided limits, not just f(c).
A common error is checking only that f(c) is defined. You must also confirm lim(x->c-) f(x) = lim(x->c+) f(x) = f(c). Intuitively, f is continuous at c if its graph passes through c without a break, jump, or hole.
keyMoves: Compute the left-hand and right-hand limits at c
Check both limits are equal (so the limit exists) and equal f(c)
Declare continuity at c only if limit = f(c)
misconceptions: Checking only that f(c) is defined, ignoring the limit
Forgetting both one-sided limits must match

nodes.title: Define a continuous function
nodes.brief: Recognise a continuous function as one continuous at every point of its domain.
nodes.quote: A real function *f* is said to be continuous if it is continuous at every point in the domain of *f*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Continuous everywhere on the domain.
A function f is continuous (as a whole) if it is continuous at EVERY point of its domain, not just at one point. A single point of continuity is not enough to call the function continuous.

PART 2 — Handle interval end points with one-sided limits; know the standard continuous families.
On a closed interval [a,b], require continuity at every interior point and at the end points using the appropriate one-sided limit. Polynomials, rational functions (wherever the denominator is non-zero), and the standard trig functions are all continuous on their domains.
keyMoves: Check continuity at every point of the domain
Handle interval end points with one-sided limits
Declare f continuous only if it is continuous everywhere on its domain
misconceptions: Calling a function continuous from continuity at a single point
Ignoring points of discontinuity outside a chosen interval

nodes.title: Combine continuous functions algebraically
nodes.brief: Use that sums, differences, products and quotients (denominator nonzero) of continuous functions are continuous.
nodes.quote: Suppose f and g be two real functions continuous at a real number c.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Sums, differences, products of continuous functions are continuous.
If f and g are both continuous at c, then f + g, f - g, and f*g are all continuous at c. So you can build continuity of complicated expressions from simple continuous building blocks (constants, x, sin x, etc.).

PART 2 — Quotients are continuous only where the denominator is non-zero.
f/g is continuous at c provided g(c) != 0. This is the key caveat: a rational function is continuous everywhere EXCEPT where its denominator vanishes. For example (x+1)/(x-2) is continuous for all x except x = 2.
keyMoves: Identify continuous building-block functions
Combine them by +, -, x to keep continuity
For a quotient, require the denominator to be non-zero at the point
misconceptions: Forgetting the g(c) not 0 condition for the quotient
Assuming a combination is discontinuous when its pieces are continuous

nodes.title: Use continuity of a composite function
nodes.brief: Apply: if g is continuous at c and f is continuous at g(c), then f o g is continuous at c.
nodes.quote: If g is continuous at c and if f is continuous at g(c), then  $(f \circ g)$  is continuous at c.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Composite continuity: inner continuous at c, outer continuous at g(c).
If g is continuous at c AND f is continuous at g(c), then the composite (f o g)(x) = f(g(x)) is continuous at c. The outer function must be checked at the OUTPUT of the inner function, g(c), not at c.

PART 2 — Apply to decompose complicated functions.
Write the function as a composite and check each piece. Example: sin(x^2) is f(g(x)) with g(x)=x^2 (continuous) and f(u)=sin u (continuous). Both are continuous, so sin(x^2) is continuous everywhere. The common error is checking f at c instead of at g(c).
keyMoves: Express the function as a composite f o g
Check g is continuous at c and f is continuous at g(c)
Conclude f o g is continuous at c
misconceptions: Checking f at the wrong point instead of at g(c)
Assuming a composite of continuous functions could be discontinuous

nodes.title: Define the derivative of a function
nodes.brief: Define f'(c) as the limit of the difference quotient as h tends to 0.
nodes.quote: The derivative of f at c is defined by
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The derivative is the limit of the difference quotient.
f'(c) = lim(h->0) [f(c+h) - f(c)] / h, provided this limit exists. The quotient [f(c+h) - f(c)]/h is the average rate of change over a small interval h; its limit as h->0 is the instantaneous rate of change.

PART 2 — The derivative as a function, and notation.
Letting c vary gives the derivative function f'(x) = lim(h->0) [f(x+h) - f(x)]/h, also written dy/dx or y'. The process of finding it is differentiation. Always keep the 'provided the limit exists' caution — at some points the limit may fail to exist.
keyMoves: Form the difference quotient [f(c + h) - f(c)] / h
Take the limit as h tends to 0
If the limit exists, call it f'(c); the rule x -> f'(x) is the derivative function
misconceptions: Forgetting the 'provided the limit exists' caution
Confusing the average rate (difference quotient) with the instantaneous derivative

nodes.title: Test differentiability at a point
nodes.brief: f is differentiable at c if the difference-quotient limit exists; equivalently both one-sided derivatives agree.
nodes.quote: we say that a function f is differentiable at a point c in its domain if both
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Differentiable means the difference-quotient limit exists.
f is differentiable at c if lim(h->0)[f(c+h)-f(c)]/h exists. Equivalently, the left-hand derivative (h->0-) and right-hand derivative (h->0+) both exist and are EQUAL. If they differ, f is not differentiable at c.

PART 2 — Corners break differentiability.
A classic example: f(x) = |x| at x=0. The left derivative is -1 and the right derivative is +1; since -1 != +1, |x| is not differentiable at 0 (even though it is continuous there). Sharp corners are points of non-differentiability.
keyMoves: Compute the left-hand and right-hand derivative limits at c
Check they exist and are equal
Declare differentiable at c only when the single limit exists
misconceptions: Assuming a continuous function is automatically differentiable
Ignoring a corner where one-sided derivatives differ (e.g. |x| at 0)

nodes.title: Use that differentiable implies continuous
nodes.brief: Apply the corollary: every differentiable function is continuous (but not conversely).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Differentiable implies continuous.
If f is differentiable at c, then f is continuous at c. So differentiability is the STRONGER condition: whenever a function has a derivative at a point, it is automatically continuous there. Use this to conclude continuity quickly.

PART 2 — The converse is FALSE.
Continuous does NOT imply differentiable. |x| is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at 0 (corner). So you may go differentiable => continuous, but never continuous => differentiable. A non-differentiable point (like a corner) can still be a point of continuity.
keyMoves: From differentiability at c, conclude continuity at c
Do NOT reverse the implication
Cite |x| at 0 as continuous-but-not-differentiable
misconceptions: Believing continuous implies differentiable
Thinking a non-differentiable point cannot be a continuous point

nodes.title: Differentiate composite functions by the chain rule
nodes.brief: Use df/dx = (dv/dt)(dt/dx) for f = v o u with t = u(x).
nodes.quote: Let *f* be a real valued function which is a composite of two
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Differentiate outer-by-inner, then multiply by the inner derivative.
For a composite f = v o u with t = u(x), df/dx = (dv/dt)(dt/dx). Differentiate the outer function with respect to the inner variable t, then multiply by dt/dx (the derivative of the inner function). Example: f(x) = sin(x^2), put t = x^2: df/dx = cos(t) * 2x = 2x cos(x^2).

PART 2 — Do not forget the inner derivative; extend to deeper composites.
The most common error is stopping at cos(x^2) and forgetting the *2x factor. For three layers, multiply all the link derivatives: d/dx sin(cos(x^2)) = cos(cos(x^2)) * (-sin(x^2)) * 2x.
keyMoves: Identify the inner function t = u(x) and outer function v
Compute dv/dt and dt/dx
Multiply: df/dx = (dv/dt)(dt/dx)
misconceptions: Forgetting to multiply by the derivative of the inner function
Differentiating the outer function with respect to x instead of t

nodes.title: Differentiate implicit functions
nodes.brief: Differentiate both sides of an implicit relation w.r.t. x, treating y as a function of x via the chain rule.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Differentiate both sides w.r.t. x, treating y as a function of x.
When y is given implicitly (the relation is not solved for y), differentiate both sides with respect to x. Every term in y picks up a dy/dx factor by the chain rule: d/dx(y^2) = 2y dy/dx, d/dx(sin y) = cos y dy/dx.

PART 2 — Collect dy/dx terms and solve algebraically.
After differentiating, gather all terms containing dy/dx on one side, factor out dy/dx, and divide to isolate it. Example: x^2 + y^2 = 25 gives 2x + 2y dy/dx = 0, so dy/dx = -x/y. The main error is forgetting the dy/dx factor on a y-term.
keyMoves: Differentiate both sides of the relation with respect to x
Apply the chain rule to terms in y (introduce dy/dx)
Solve the equation algebraically for dy/dx
misconceptions: Forgetting the dy/dx factor when differentiating a term in y
Trying to solve the relation for y first when it is not necessary

nodes.title: Differentiate inverse trigonometric functions
nodes.brief: Use the chain rule on inverse trig functions to obtain their derivatives.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Derive inverse-trig derivatives by implicit differentiation.
Write y = arcsin x, so sin y = x. Differentiate both sides: cos y dy/dx = 1, so dy/dx = 1/cos y = 1/sqrt(1 - sin^2 y) = 1/sqrt(1 - x^2). This gives the standard result d/dx(arcsin x) = 1/sqrt(1 - x^2).

PART 2 — Standard results and chain-rule use.
Key derivatives: d/dx(arcsin x) = 1/sqrt(1-x^2), d/dx(arccos x) = -1/sqrt(1-x^2), d/dx(arctan x) = 1/(1+x^2). For composites, apply the chain rule: d/dx arctan(x^2) = 1/(1+x^4) * 2x. Do NOT confuse d/dx(arcsin x) with 1/cos x.
keyMoves: Write y = (inverse trig) of x and convert to the direct trig relation
Differentiate implicitly / by chain rule to isolate dy/dx
Use the standard inverse-trig derivative formulas in larger problems
misconceptions: Confusing the derivative of sin-inverse x with 1/cos x
Dropping the chain-rule factor for a composite inverse-trig expression

nodes.title: Define the exponential function
nodes.brief: Recognise the exponential function with base b > 1, with domain R and positive range.
nodes.quote: The exponential function with positive base b > 1 is the function
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — y = b^x (b > 1): domain all reals, range positive reals.
The exponential function with base b > 1 is y = b^x. Its domain is all of R, its range is the POSITIVE reals (b^x is always > 0, never zero or negative), it passes through (0, 1) since b^0 = 1, and it is strictly increasing.

PART 2 — The natural exponential e^x.
With base e (a constant between 2 and 3) we get y = e^x, the natural exponential. It is special in calculus because it is unchanged by differentiation: d/dx(e^x) = e^x. Note b^x can never be negative or zero.
keyMoves: Recognise y = b^x with b > 1 as an exponential function
State its domain R and range the positive reals
Single out the natural exponential e^x with base e
misconceptions: Thinking the exponential function can be negative or zero
Confusing the base b with the variable x

nodes.title: Define the logarithm as the inverse of the exponential
nodes.brief: Define log_b a = x to mean b^x = a; the log function is the inverse of the exponential.
nodes.quote: we say logarithm of *a* to base *b* is *x* if  $b^x = a$ .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Logarithm is the inverse of the exponential: log_b a = x means b^x = a.
For base b > 1, log_b a = x is defined to mean b^x = a. So the log answers 'to what power must b be raised to get a?'. Example: log_2 8 = 3 because 2^3 = 8. Converting between log and exponential form is the key skill.

PART 2 — Domain is the positive reals; default base is e here.
The argument of a logarithm must be positive — log of zero or a negative number is undefined. In this chapter log x means the NATURAL logarithm (base e), so log e = 1 and log 1 = 0.
keyMoves: Translate log_b a = x into the exponential statement b^x = a
Restrict the argument of log to positive reals
Use base e (natural log) when the base is unspecified in this chapter
misconceptions: Taking the logarithm of a non-positive number
Confusing log_b a with a^b or b^a

nodes.title: Differentiate e^x and log x
nodes.brief: Use d/dx(e^x) = e^x and d/dx(log x) = 1/x.
nodes.quote: The derivative of  $e^x$  w.r.t., x is  $e^x$ ; i.e.,  $\frac{d}{dx}(e^x) = e^x$ .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The two key derivatives: d/dx(e^x) = e^x and d/dx(log x) = 1/x.
The natural exponential is unchanged by differentiation: d/dx(e^x) = e^x. The natural logarithm differentiates to d/dx(log x) = 1/x. These are memorised base results.

PART 2 — Combine with the chain rule for composites.
For e^(g(x)) use e^(g(x)) * g'(x); for log(g(x)) use g'(x)/g(x). Examples: d/dx(e^(2x)) = 2e^(2x); d/dx(log(x^2)) = 2x/x^2 = 2/x. The common error is writing d/dx(e^x) = x e^(x-1) (treating it as a power) — e^x is NOT a power function.
keyMoves: Use d/dx(e^x) = e^x
Use d/dx(log x) = 1/x
Combine with the chain rule for composite exponential/log expressions
misconceptions: Writing d/dx(e^x) = x e^(x-1) as if it were a power
Forgetting the chain-rule factor for e^(g(x)) or log(g(x))

nodes.title: Differentiate using logarithmic differentiation
nodes.brief: Take logs of both sides before differentiating, especially for variable-base-variable-exponent forms.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Take logs of both sides, then differentiate.
Logarithmic differentiation handles y = (f(x))^(g(x)) with BOTH base and exponent variable (the power rule and exponential rule both fail here). Take log of both sides: log y = g(x) log f(x). Then differentiate: the left side gives (1/y) dy/dx by the chain rule.

PART 2 — Solve for dy/dx and substitute y back.
After differentiating, multiply through by y to isolate dy/dx, then replace y with the original (f(x))^(g(x)). Example: y = x^x gives log y = x log x, so (1/y) dy/dx = log x + 1, hence dy/dx = x^x (log x + 1). The common error is treating a variable exponent as a constant power.
keyMoves: Take log of both sides: log y = g(x) log f(x)
Differentiate both sides w.r.t. x, using (1/y) dy/dx on the left
Solve for dy/dx and substitute y back
misconceptions: Treating a variable exponent as a constant power
Forgetting the (1/y) dy/dx factor from differentiating log y

nodes.title: Differentiate functions in parametric form
nodes.brief: For x = f(t), y = g(t), find dy/dx = (dy/dt) / (dx/dt).
nodes.quote: is said to be parametric form with t as a parameter.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Differentiate x and y separately with respect to t.
When x = f(t) and y = g(t) (parametric form, t the parameter), compute dx/dt and dy/dt each in terms of t.

PART 2 — Form dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt), not the reciprocal.
The slope is dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt), provided dx/dt is not 0. Example: x = t^2, y = t^3 gives dx/dt = 2t, dy/dt = 3t^2, so dy/dx = 3t^2/2t = 3t/2. The common error is inverting it to (dx/dt)/(dy/dt) — dy/dt goes on TOP.
keyMoves: Differentiate x = f(t) and y = g(t) with respect to t
Form dy/dx = (dy/dt) / (dx/dt)
Require dx/dt not 0
misconceptions: Computing dy/dx as (dx/dt)/(dy/dt) (inverted)
Differentiating y with respect to x directly without the parameter

nodes.title: Find the second order derivative
nodes.brief: Differentiate the first derivative again to obtain d2y/dx2, using the full differentiation toolkit.
nodes.quote: which is called the *second order derivative* of y w.r.t. x
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Differentiate twice: the second derivative is d/dx of the first derivative.
The second-order derivative d2y/dx2 (also f''(x), y'', or y_2) is found by differentiating dy/dx again with respect to x. Step 1: find dy/dx using whatever technique fits (chain, product, implicit, parametric, log, exp/log). Step 2: differentiate that result once more.

PART 2 — Differentiate, do not square.
The second derivative is d/dx(dy/dx), NOT (dy/dx)^2. Re-apply product and chain rules when differentiating the first derivative. Example: y = x^4 gives dy/dx = 4x^3, then d2y/dx2 = 12x^2. This capstone draws on every differentiation rule in the chapter.
keyMoves: Find the first derivative dy/dx with the appropriate technique
Differentiate dy/dx again with respect to x
Report d2y/dx2 (= f''(x))
misconceptions: Squaring the first derivative instead of differentiating it again
Forgetting to re-apply product/chain rules when differentiating dy/dx
